speaker: interviewer
text: --Grodek?

speaker: interviewee
text: [COUGHING] Am I allowed to cough, sir?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, you can cough as much you like.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: It's free of charge.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't need a lot. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: Let us talk now very quickly about the Gymnasium, the secondary school.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, it has no special name, and it was on [NON-ENGLISH] and was mixed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it again a situation of Jewish students being a minority?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were not many Jewish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Minority, a strong minority?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, of course, not many.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Ukrainians as well in the Gymnasium?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, they were all mixed. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was life like with the other students? Was there peace and harmony, or were there any conflicts?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no conflicts. No, no. There were some among even the students, anti-Semites. There were some. But they couldn't do it aloud because it was not allowed. [BACKGROUND NOISE] No. After-- and especially after the Constitution [NON-ENGLISH]. You know Constitution [NON-ENGLISH]-- [NON-ENGLISH]. So everybody-- it was people all the same. There was no-- no difference.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was much better later. Yes. So no conflicts at all. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the teachers?

speaker: interviewee
text: The teachers, there was some anti-Semitic, but they didn't-- it wasn't-- it was covered up. They wouldn't say anything, no, nothing against the students. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about--

speaker: interviewee
text: But, sorry, there were not many Jewish students. And especially for [BACKGROUND NOISE] you had to go on Shabbat on Saturday, and some, you know, the parents, who were a little bit more religious, they wouldn't allow it. But I knew of-- I knew of some. My sister-in-law-- my younger-- she was younger than I was-- my late husband's sister-- was there too, but she was younger. And so her grandfather was very religious, Orthodox. But she used to go on Saturday too.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how--

speaker: interviewee
text: She's not alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did that work? The usual school day, when did it start and when did it finish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: The normal school day at Gymnasium, when did you start school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Normally. Normally, like-- like everywhere else.

speaker: interviewer
text: 8 o'clock in the morning? 9 o'clock?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was 8 o'clock, not 9 o'clock.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you finished what time?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a break during the day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, [NON-ENGLISH]. Yes, there were breaks.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was that five days or six days a week? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: It was all week.

speaker: interviewer
text: All week.

speaker: interviewee
text: Normal, till Friday.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Saturday?

speaker: interviewee
text: Friday-- Saturday too, yes, but we had to, but no Sunday. We had to go on Saturday.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- Did most Jewish students stay at home on Saturdays?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we used to go to organizations, not at home.

speaker: interviewer
text: But the school, to Gymnasium.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, the school, yes, of course we-- on Saturday, we had to after noon, after school, so we left and did our things at home, whatever we did.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, I was asking about the fact that a lot of Jewish students didn't go to school on Saturday because the parents wouldn't let them.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they wouldn't-- they wouldn't be allowed to come to the-- to go into Gymnasium.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were not-- Saturday was a must. The-- you couldn't be exempted. It's impossible.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they have a--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was not possible, no. So they couldn't go to Gymnasium. Some even I know of them, they were a bit more religious. They studied at home privately. The same teachers from high school went home to them-- they paid-- and they studied at home. I know of some cases like-- I knew then.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were going to Gymnasium, was there any difference in expectations of girls compared to boys?

speaker: interviewee
text: To-- to-- after the war?

speaker: interviewer
text: No, before the war. In other words, were boys expected to do more or less than girls? Or were different things expected of boys at-- at Gymnasium?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know of anything different. I mean, what you learned there, you learned even now in Gymnasium.

speaker: interviewer
text: I-- I guess I'm-- I'm--

speaker: interviewee
text: There was even a language-- external language.

speaker: interviewer
text: I guess I'm asking that were girls expected to finish Gymnasium before the war, in Grodek? Was there a general expectation that girls finish Gymnasium? Or that wasn't very important?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went there to finish it. There were no special expectations.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that normal for your girlfriends?

speaker: interviewee
text: [COUGHS] Not all. Some didn't finish it. You had to pay for it, and some couldn't afford, and they left it earlier. I finished it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you-- what did you want to do after you finished Gymnasium?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that's a good question. I wanted to study medicine. And even now, I love it. Wherever-- my doctor gives me sometimes literature to read about it. I wanted-- I wanted very badly, you know, medicine. I always talked of it. I remember back, women's [INAUDIBLE]. First of all, university was very expensive. Women, especially Jewish women, were not admitted at all to universities.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was maybe-- I think it was anti-Semitism in Poland. I knew only one doctor-- when I was at nursing school, I know one woman, Dr. Rosenthal. I still remember the name from then, one woman doctor who studied in Poland. Otherwise, I think there could be one or two normal women. Men, not many they-- you know, a lot of doctors here even, I know a number, that they started in France or in Italy, Jewish doctors. They were-- they didn't-- you know, they didn't admit many Jews, I think because of anti-Semitism. That was the problem. And women, hardly one or two. And I remember, as a child, I played-- I was about 15 or 16, at school still, and with a girlfriend. I played something, I don't know, ball or whatever, outdoors. And I fell, and I didn't realize it. They said it was a-- a broken bottle, a piece of glass. I still have the scar. I fell on it and cut my leg right through from here to here. I still have the scar. And the-- because the scar wouldn't be so big, a wide scar, but because it was operated. There was a small hospital there, and my-- my mother called in a doctor. He was our house doctor, friend to doctor. I still remember his name-- Dr. [? Margoles. ?] And he was killed on Janowska, in Lwow, in the camp there. He was a-- he had a wife and a daughter. They all were killed. And my-- my mother called him. He came over. And as he seen me, he said, too much-- you lose too much blood, told to my mother. You can't take her to-- a small hospital was there. And to Lwow, to a larger hospital, there was no time at all. It took quite a long time by train or by carriage and horses.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he said, I have to operate it here in your place. In the dining room, he sat me. They took me up on a table, and I had to hold my leg. And the leg-- because it was cut, the-- you know, it was cut so so the muscles on top were pushed up, the other one down, and I have seen the white bone in the center. I wasn't scared; I've seen it. My mother fainted. My brother fainted. He said, out, out, out. He threw them all out. He was left with me, the doctor.

speaker: interviewee
text: I tell you why I came to nursing. And he said, you, little girl, you-- you have a lot of courage. I can see that. You're not crying at all. He said, you-- you sit on the table. Hold your leg here. Because it comes up and down. And as we cut through, so you know, it comes, you know, like-- like this. Hold it tight here, as tight as you can, and I will sew it together without anything-- no anesthetic, nothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in shock. If you're in shock, you don't feel pain. Hold it just [INAUDIBLE], he told me like-- like this, and it won't jump-- you can see it jumping, your leg-- and I will sew it together. No one was there, only me and myself and him. I held it like this, and he sew it together. And I looked, and I looked here and there. And he said, it's done. You have a lot of courage. Would you like to be a nurse? I said, no, a doctor. So he said, a doctor? I don't think it's possible. But a nurse, I'll-- my mother didn't think nowhere to go about it. I was in Lwow. My mother was in Grodek. He took me to Lwow and wrote me. He was with me there and said, that girl will be a good nurse. And I was. And that because of this.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, they put me to bed. And my mother came back, and he told her about it. He said, that girl will be a nurse. I'll make from her a nurse. I didn't think of it then. He put me to bed, and I had to stay in bed until it heals and the muscles heal together. And I had to take-- he said, it will take about-- until she will be a little better-- two or three weeks.

speaker: interviewee
text: But two days later, we had a woman-- a peasant woman-- coming, because there were floors-- wooden floors only polished, you know. She did the floor, and I was in bed, and I looked at her. And she left. And I said, Mom, would you give me a bucket of water and a rag? She didn't clean enough, so it was easy floor. [LAUGHS] What are you doing? You can't go with that like that. I said it again. I can't-- she went-- she said, no, I won't let you. And I said, and I will cry until you let me. And my mother went out, and I took the rag. And the leg-- it was after the operation-- I hold it like this, and the other one on the knee, and went down the bed, and I washed the floor. That's me. I can't stand it. That's why I haven't got a helper here. And I did it. And when he came back, my mother told him what she did. I will kill her. I didn't do it. He laughed. Then we went to Lwow-- Lwow, and he wrote me. And that's how. But medicine, I dreamt of it always. I still like it, and I know exactly everything what goes on [INAUDIBLE]. And my doctor always says, why didn't you study medicine? Couldn't. It was impossible. So that's why I studied-- I did nursing. In your final year at Gymnasium, were there many girls in the class?

speaker: interviewee
text: Any what?

speaker: interviewer
text: Many girls? Were there just as many girls as boys?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not many. No. You mean Jewish?

speaker: interviewer
text: No, in total, in general.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, some were left behind. Still, we were quite-- I don't remember exactly how many, 18 or 20. That was all.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you moved to Lwow from Grodek. You left Grodek?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, because there were no nursing schools in Grodek. I had to go to Lwow. That's why-- why I give my second address. Since then, I lived in-- I lived in Lwow.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long-- how long did you have to wait before you started study at Lwow? How much time was there--

speaker: interviewee
text: To nursing? No, I think 17, 18. There were some accepted not with finished high school. But I think there were some accepted. Here, they-- they-- most of them have high school here. But there, not all of them. I think it was the sixth form they accepted. It means mala matura. In Polish, it was mala matura. Sixth form was mala matura. They were accepted.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many years' study was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nursing? Three years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you started in 1935?

speaker: interviewee
text: '36, I think, because it finished just-- '37, I got married and I still haven't finished it. I finished it because when the Russians came, it was in '39 that the war started, '39. And '37, I finished it, yes, and got-- and got married then.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were your nursing studies like? What do you remember most from them?

speaker: interviewee
text: All the subjects you had to study, you know, within--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a hard life?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was hard, yes, because we had lectures and doctors at the hospital every day. And we even had our meals at the hospital. And very often, we had to get up very early. And you see, it was oral and practical. We had to-- as we studied, we had to work straightaway. And it's every shift for the work night duty. So I work night time. In day, I didn't have time when to sleep, because we had-- had the lectures. It was very hard. I think it's much easier here now. But it was hard, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were there many Jewish people studying the nursing with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were only Jews. We were only-- it was at the Jewish hospital. And that Jewish hospital is famous. It was on the-- oh, I knew the street and I forgot it, the street. Not [NON-ENGLISH]. I will call it. It's on the tip of my tongue, but I can't say it. And it was a big Jewish hospital. There was a Jewish hospital and mostly Jewish doctors and professors, and you know. One we had, he was Professor [? Pesik ?]. He was-- he was Jewish but from Czechoslovakia. And I remember, there was a charge sister. It was very clean. And the patient had to be-- the bed changed every day. And Professor [? Pesik ?] was very particular, very clean. And I remember the charge sister. Each charge sister had under her wings a few students, about four or five, in-- in one ward. You know, they were like here, wards, a hospital like here. It's the same. And she fed-- that sister herself fed a patient in bed, lying. And she had to feed her with a egg, soft egg.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she dropped and-- there was-- it dropped from the spoon. She fed her, and she dropped a drop of yellow egg yolk on the cover. And it was there. And Dr. [? Pesik ?] came in. He said, who did that? And she showed to me. She did it. She didn't want to say she did it.

speaker: interviewee
text: I couldn't do anything against. And I was afraid to say no, it's her. But she did not-- I-- I couldn't say anything. He said, once more, and we get you out of  the school. I never forget that. I never forget it. She didn't want to say that she did it herself. He was very particular, very clean. And we had to look after the patients they had. If he seen a-- a small stain, you couldn't do anything about it. He-- he-- he had seen it straightaway. He  was an old man. He was Professor [? Pesik ?] from Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- so we had a hard life, see? I think it's easier here, much easier, because night duty was about four times in a month. And the next day, we couldn't sleep much, because we-- here, they have-- after night duty, they have a day free. We didn't work in the morning, but we have in the afternoon, the lectures. So I had to come back. Did you have--

speaker: interviewee
text: And I lived in a Jewish dormitory, and that was far from the school, from the hospital. Have to walk there every day, not by train, to walk. [CLEARS THROAT]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any contact with the general people-- general population in Lwow at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, there were only Jews in the hospital admitted.

speaker: interviewer
text: Outside the hospital.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no others. No, no, no. Yes, because once a week we all had to go to-- I told you before-- to the hospital-- to the general hospital, not the Jewish, which was very, very far. That's why I brought my child-- I told you-- over there. And there were lectures too. So we went once-- once, twice in a month, we had to go over there, and to listen to lectures, and see around how it is, you know, with patients, everything. But otherwise, no contact, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any difference in Lwow compared to Grodek with the attitude of the general population towards Jewish people, or was it the same? In Grodek, you had-- you said that there were some--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, but, of course. But I didn't come in contact with other people. We were mostly-- we lived near [INAUDIBLE] hospital. But I'll tell you about this hospital. It was known. At that hospital, there were only Jewish. And it was always full of patients, because there were always a lot of sick people then, more than-- maybe more than today. The hospital was always full. And one of my uncles-- my father's sister, a younger sister, only sister-- from Grodek, he was her husband. He was brought there to the hospital. I don't know what he'd suffered from. But he was sick and had to be at the hospital.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they brought him from Grodek to this hospital, and he was there quite for a long time. And it happened that that was just before the ghetto was closed. The ghetto was already there, established. Otherwise, he wouldn't go to a hospital. During the German occupation, people were not-- Jewish people were not admitted to hospitals. Jewish-- even the Jewish one was closed. But he was from before there, and it was full of his other patients. And the Germans-- it was end of '92. I think the-- the ghetto was closed already in-- in beginning of '93, if I remember exactly. And he was still at the hospital. You know what they did? They locked the hospital all around and burned it alive with all the patients. That was known in Lwow.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know if anyone told the story. They went-- I can't read a lot-- not a lot, not a bit about in Lwow. There is Warszawa, there is Lodz, there is Lublin that I read about, but not about the Lwow march. And the hospital, [INAUDIBLE] with the patients alive, was set on fire.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can we go back to when you just finished your nursing qualifications?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do next?

speaker: interviewee
text: When?

speaker: interviewee
text: After? I finished nursing, you know, with the three years additional with nursing. So I finished right to '37, to my marriage. And then '39, my daughter was born.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about between '37 and '39? What did you-- did you work at all somewhere?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. You know pregnancy takes nine months. It's nearly another years, so I didn't have much time. No. Nor-- during pregnancy, I didn't work. And after, we had the Soviets, '39. She was actually born into the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the Soviets came, and I-- I couldn't work in my profession. I didn't know Russian. So I didn't work as a-- but with the Soviets, the child was in a-- how did they call it? For not a full time, from-- to say from 9:00 to 3:00. [NON-ENGLISH], you know what is [NON-ENGLISH]. Like here, they have the child care. Daycare.

speaker: interviewee
text: Something like it. It was different there. And I had to go to work. So the polytechnic was-- that's where my brother studied-- my husband. Polytechnic was on the [NON-ENGLISH]. I remember the street, [NON-ENGLISH]. It was converted during the Russian-- when the Russians-- Soviets were-- in a month, it was converted to a laboratorium, lab, you know, laboratory-- a medical laboratory. And they assigned me there to work in the laboratory instead of hospitals. They didn't have enough people, so--

speaker: interviewer
text: That was after the war broke out.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was when the Russians came in. The Russians were from-- because first, we had the Germans, and they changed positions, since the Russians-- and-- and they-- pushed them out. And the communists were at the eastern side of Poland. It went fro and back.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went into-- I didn't work in nursing. In nursing, I started to work after war-- after the war, in the DP camps. I put it down there. That's right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I don't think that I put down that I didn't work there. It's written, 'What did you work before?' It's a mistake, because I didn't work in-- and I didn't write it down-- not in nursing, but during the Soviet-- Soviet occupation in a laboratorium.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when the war actually started, what are your first memories of the war starting involve?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that in '39?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember the month. Somebody must have--

speaker: interviewer
text: September.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I think it's September. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what--

speaker: interviewee
text: And my daughter was born in March, 15 of March.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what are your first memories? Were there soldiers-- Russian soldiers coming in, or German soldiers?

speaker: interviewee
text: When the war started? No, not the Germans, the Russians-- communists then-- came in 1940.

speaker: interviewer
text: At the beginning, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: At the beginning, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: In winter.

speaker: interviewee
text: First, they did bomb. The Germans bombed. I remember exactly when Lwow was bombed. And the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that at the end of '39?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, that's right. It was bombing. We didn't know who, these or the others. We were first heavily bombed, you know. And then we had-- they said that the Germans are coming, but the Russians came from the  other side. And we-- Lwow falls under Soviet occupation.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when did the Russians first come?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think in 1940. In '40, we had the Russians.

speaker: interviewer
text: During winter?

speaker: interviewee
text: From '40-- I think they stayed over a year with us, till the Germans pushed them out and came back. So it was since '40 to-- over '41.

speaker: interviewer
text: From winter '40? January, February '40?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember the months, exactly. But I know from '40 to '41, we had the Russians. They-- they even took over people's homes, because a lot came with wives. They took over my home too. I was left only with one room. They took everything away. I mean, they putting people there. They had full of people. So they didn't ask you. They just took away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Apart from those important changes of taking property, what other changes did the Russians make when they came?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it was very hard to obtain food, very hard, food to obtain. We had to stand in-- in the long queues, or we had children or no children, we still had to stand for-- for days in queues to get a little bit of, I think a few grams of sugar or bread or anything. Food was very hard to obtain, unless-- it was called-- on the black market, it was called [NON-ENGLISH], who had money and could get it privately, you know, aside. Otherwise, it was on-- we had to wait in long queues for-- for bread.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the attitude of the Russians to the Jewish population?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, no, nothing particular. But it was something else. Who was not-- you see, the Germans came from the other side, because I think Lodz was already occupied in [INAUDIBLE] by the Germans. And they came to other-- to our side. And we still had-- were under the Russians. And we were scared. We didn't know. We thought the Russians were here.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the people fled from the-- before the Germans came in, fled from the other side of Poland, from the west. We were in the east. Lwow is in east Poland. Fled to-- to our side, to Lwow. They were called [NON-ENGLISH]. You've heard of it? [NON-ENGLISH] They fled from their places-- Refugees.

speaker: interviewee
text: --where the Germans were already occupied, because west Poland was earlier occupied by the Germans. They came from Germany. And east was still and the Russians came from the other side. So they fled from there to our place. But they didn't know that the-- the Russians are here, the communists. And the Russians didn't want them, so they-- oh, it was awful. They used to come at night time, and people were asleep, take the people-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] all the people who arrived there who didn't have-- we had passports. But the Soviets, we used to have special passports that we-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that we lived here before. Other people couldn't obtain those passports. These had arrived from the other places. So they were called [NON-ENGLISH]. And the Russians took them forcibly at night. There were screams on the street. I remember at night time, the street screams. I'll tell you something about it later. They took them forcibly to Russia-- and everybody knew-- to the [NON-ENGLISH]. And no one wanted to go there. That's why they hid there and around, and hid here and there. But they couldn't hide, because the Russians were everywhere. So they took them forcibly to Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were transported to-- to Russia. But my mother could foresee what happens. When she came to Lwow to us from Grodek, she used to say, they don't know the people. I wish they would take me forcibly to Russia. That something worse will come, she could see it before. I-- I believe that If they would take me and all my children, the whole family, I would go. I would ask them to take us too. But they didn't take anyone. They took only the [NON-ENGLISH], those people from there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. We'll continue the story in the next tape.
